questions.questionText: Narcolepsy is characterized by a classic tetrad of:
questions.optionB: Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy (sudden loss of muscle tone triggered by emotions), sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations
questions.optionC: Snoring, apnea, restless legs, periodic limb movements
questions.optionD: Vivid nightmares, night terrors, sleepwalking, enuresis
questions.explanation: Core concept: Narcolepsy tetrad = EDS + cataplexy + sleep paralysis + hypnagogic hallucinations. Why correct: Narcolepsy type 1 (with cataplexy): deficiency of hypocretin/orexin in hypothalamus. Pathognomonic: cataplexy (emotion-triggered atonia while awake). Sleep paralysis: inability to move during sleep-wake transitions. Hypnagogic: hallucinations at sleep onset. EDS: irresistible sleep attacks. Why A wrong: Anxiety symptoms. Why C wrong: These describe obstructive sleep apnea + RLS/PLMS. Why D wrong: These are parasomnias (NREM parasomnias: night terrors, sleepwalking; REM: nightmare disorder). Clinical relevance: Diagnosis: MSLT (Multiple Sleep Latency Test) — mean sleep latency ≤8 min + ≥2 SOREMPs. Treatment: modafinil for EDS; sodium oxybate for cataplexy; venlafaxine/TCAs for cataplexy. Exam trap: Complete tetrad present in only ~30% — EDS is universal, cataplexy most specific. Memory aid: 'Narcolepsy = Napping + (cata)Plexy + Paralysis + hallucinations at Night onset.' Reference: Ahuja – Sleep Disorders.
